subtitle: Exploring the Evolution of India’s Carnivory from the Shastras to the Guptas
title: India’s Relationship with Meat—II
description: Exploring the Evolution of India’s Carnivory from the Shastras to the Guptas
body_html:
In a previous article we studied the Vedic and post-Vedic positions on meat as food before the Common Era. We concluded with the understanding that meat was far more ubiquitous in the Indian diet at the time than we like to admit today. That the injunction on meat was really a spectrum with some verses prohibiting it all, others allowing some, and yet others allowing it all. Precisely as it is today.

However, this spectrum had already started a slow shift in favor of higher prohibitions toward the end of the Upanishadic period. Even as Yajnavalkya admits to his love for beef, for instance, he still warns us against it, lest one become the bearer of a grave sin and be reborn as a “strange being.” Was this a Shramanic influence? Did the Buddha’s emphasis on nonviolence and karma inform the Upanishadic ideals in this regard? That’s the notion much of mainstream scholarship operates on. It’s therefore a good idea to ascertain if that really is the case. And if at all that weren’t the case, the question one cannot deny that Hindus are visibly less meat inclined today than they were back in the day. There has to be a point where something changed drastically and that point is not the Upanishads.

We know that Hinduism underwent its most significant codification in the early centuries AD—a process often described as the śāstrification of Vedic philosophy. Although it likely began under the Kushans, it reached its fullest expression under the Guptas. This period witnessed the rise of a new body of literature known as the Dharmaśāstras, the most prominent of which is the Mānava Dharmaśāstra, better known as the Manusmṛti.

To understand the status of meat during the Common Era, it is essential to examine Manusmṛti’s view on food. That is our starting point here. For a more comprehensive picture, however, we will also consider how meat was perceived in society under the Kushans, the Śakas, the Guptas, and in the post-Gupta India. Although pre–Common Era dietary practices were discussed in a previous article, we will briefly revisit that period to address one crucial omission—the Mauryas. This will include Kauṭilya’s Arthaśāstra alongside Buddhist and Jain sources, to finally clarify the question of Shramanic nonviolence in relation to meat consumption.

The Era of the Shastras

In the fifth adhyāya or chapter, Manusmriti lays out a detailed list of things one may and may not eat. Before turning to that, however, it is worth examining a curious verse in the eleventh adhyāya, which concerns prāyaścitta, or atonement. In this chapter, Manu enumerates a range of transgressions along with their corresponding expiatory rites, some offering valuable insight into how dietary acts—especially those involving violence or impurity—were morally and ritually reconciled within the broader framework of dharma. One such verse is 11.157:1

brahmacārī tu yo’śnīyān madhu māṃsaṃ kathaṃ cana,
sa kṛtvā prākṛtaṃ kṛcchraṃ vrataśeṣaṃ samāpayet.
“If a person keeping a vow happens, in any way, to eat honey or meat, he should perform the original Kṛcchra, and then complete the remainder of his vow.” (trans. Jha)

A brahmachari was typically a student. Therefore, at the very least, we can conclude that meat was off-limits to students, Brahmins in particular. In the same chapter, we have an entire set of verses discussing cows in particular. Clearly, this animal was treated differently enough to warrant a section unto itself. These are verses 11.107 thru 115.2 In 107, the killing of cow is noted as an upapāta, a minor offense:3

upapātakasaṃyukto goghno māsaṃ yavān pibet,
kṛtavāpo vased goṣṭhe carmaṇā tena saṃvṛtaḥ.
“The cow-killer, charged with a minor offence, shall drink barley for three months; and having shaved his head and covered with the skin of the cow, he shall live in the cow-pen.” (trans. Jha)

The verses that follow list out the activities a cow-killer must perform for three months in order to atone for his sin.4 These range from bathing in cow urine to sleeping in a certain posture to living in a cow pen. At the end of the period, he’s supposed to either give away all his cows along with a bull—or if falling short, all his possessions—to anyone learned in the Vedas. Despite its designation as a “minor” offense, the killing of cows seems to attract quite a harsh penalty.

And now we can return to chapter 5 that specifically talks about diet. Curiously, 5.48 discourages meat altogether because it involves the killing of animals:5

nākṛtvā prāṇināṃ hiṃsāṃ māṃsamutpadyate kva cit,
na ca prāṇivadhaḥ svargyastasmān māṃsaṃ vivarjayet.
“Meat is never obtained without having encompassed the killing of animals; and the killing of animals does not lead to heaven; hence one should avoid meat.” (trans. Jha)

Remember the Aśvamedha—the grand horse sacrifice celebrated in the Vedas and epics as the pinnacle of royal ritualism? Manu spares not even that. In 5.53, he subverts its exalted stature by declaring that a man who abstains from meat earns the same merit as one who performs the Aśvamedha every year for a hundred years:6

varṣe varṣe’śvamedhena yo yajeta śataṃ samāḥ,
māṃsāni ca na khāded yastayoḥ puṇyaphalaṃ samam.
“If a man performs the Aśvamedha Sacrifice every year, for a hundred years—and another does not eat meat—the merit and reward of both these are the same.” (trans. Jha)

Without explicitly condemning the rite, Manu renders it morally redundant, shifting the axis of virtue from ritual grandeur to ethical restraint. 5.54 continues the theme with the declaration that abstinence from meat is more rewarding than subsistence on sacred fruits and roots:7

phalamūlāśanairmedhyairmunyannānāṃ ca bhojanaiḥ,
na tat phalamavāpnoti yatmāṃsaparivarjanāt.
“By subsisting upon sacred fruits and roots, and by eating the food of hermits, one does not obtain that reward which he does by abstaining from meat.” (trans. Jha)

There are several other instances of injunctions against meat throughout the work, most of them in the fifth discourse. Having said that, there’s also 5.56 that tempers down its stance from strong injunction to mild discouragement:8

na māṃsabhakṣaṇe doṣo na madye na ca maithune,
pravṛttireṣā bhūtānāṃ nivṛttistu mahāphalā.
“There is no sin in the eating of meat, nor in wine, nor in sexual intercourse. Such is the natural way of living beings; but abstention is conducive to great rewards.” (trans. Jha)

And this isn’t an isolated situation either. We’ve already noted a similar attitude in the case of the horse sacrifice as well. 5.53 does not ban it, it just renders it less spiritually attractive. One way to reconcile this is by admitting that meat was forbidden to the Brahmins and reluctantly permitted to others, but 5.27 poses a problem with that too:9

prokṣitaṃ bhakṣayen māṃsaṃ brāhmaṇānāṃ ca kāmyayā,
yathāvidhi niyuktastu prāṇānāmeva cātyaye.
“He may eat meat that has been consecrated; also at the wish of Brāhmaṇas; and when invited according to law; and when his life is in danger.” (trans. Jha)

This verse, along with the ones that follow, directly addresses Brahmins and allows them to partake in meat that has been “consecrated.” Besides in circumstances of extreme duress, of course. In the same section, other verses reiterate the Late Vedic mandate of offering meat in ceremonies like Śrāddha to satisfy the departed. Animals like deer, boar, tortoises, blackbuck, goat, and even rhinoceros are suggested. Of course, 5.11 prohibits certain creatures, such as carnivorous birds and “one-hoofed animals”:10

kravyādān śakunān sarvāntathā grāmanivāsinaḥ,
anirdiṣṭāṃścekaśaphān ṭiṭṭibhaṃ ca vivarjayet.
“He shall avoid all carnivorous birds, and also those living in villages, the one-hoofed animals not specified, and also the Ṭiṭṭibha.” (trans. Jha)

In sum, Manusmriti’s stance on meat sits largely in the middle-ground between “eat only certain animals” and “don’t eat any.” A compromise, if you will, through the principle that one may consume meat when ritually required, such as during funerary rites, but should abstain otherwise.

Three works form the form the foundational triad of the classical Dharmaśāstra tradition. Manusmriti is one, the other two are Yajnavalkya Smriti and Narada Smriti. Of these, the former doesn’t go into as many details on food as MS does, but in 1.33 it does drop a passing injunction against meat:11

madhumāṃsāñjanocchiṣṭaśuktastrīprāṇihiṃsanam,
bhāskarālokanāślīlaparivādādi varjayet.
“Honey, meat, anointment, leavings, harshness, women, harming animals, sight of the Sun, coarseness, and. slander, and like such, he should avoid.” (trans. Gharpure)

Just as the MS, YS too has an entire section dedicated to food mandates, chapter 7. In this section, we have 1.167–68 where “unhallowed” meat is forbidden.12 The same is repeated in 171 where unhallowed meat is likened with “objects springing from feces.”13 In the following verses, we find the exact same injunction as we did in MS 5.11 where carnivorous birds, one-hoofed animals, etc. are prohibited from consumption as food.14 Once again, we’re left with the same compromise—ideally don’t eat meat unless as part of ritual, but if you must, eat only meat from certain animals. And no beef.

To conclude, the Shastras do not prohibit meat in entirety, but we are beginning to see a clear shift toward vegetarianism by now. Hindus on the Common Era are no longer as comfortable with meat as they used to be in the times of the Vedas, the Brahmanas, the Upanishads, and the epics. The only way to explain this is an external influence. Perhaps from the Shramanas of Magadha? We’ll see in a bit but for now, let’s dwell on the Late Antiquity a bit longer. We know what the Shastras said. But we still don’t know what the society followed. Did the layman at large still avoid meat as rigorously as mandated by the books?

Early Common Era

This is the era of the Kushans, the Shakas, and the Satavahanas. Greeks too but their hegemony had already folded by 10 AD, so we won’t consider them. Of course, short of archaeology, there’s no direct way to ascertain what people really ate at the time. But a study of secular literature from the time can throw some light. While the Shastras are encumbered with an overarching sacerdotal responsibility, works of a more secular nature are not. The most readily accessible works from this corpus are the medical treatises of Charaka and Sushruta.

Chapter 27 of Charaka Samhita is dedicated to food. And a considerable section thereof discusses meat in unprecedented depth. As many as 55 verses are solely employed in the definition and categorization of various kinds of animals in the context of meat. From 27.56 onward, we are told the effects of consuming each category of meats. In 27.56, for instance, we have a class of creatures the meat of which helps improve strength and libido.15 The previous verse tells us that this class includes, among others, rodents, water animals, amphibians, and certain birds.16 The verses that follow similarly lay out the health benefits of all other classes of meat, and include not just usual suspects like sheep and goat, but also more exotic ideas like peacock, swan, parrot, porcupine, rhino, and many more, a total of no fewer than 32 verses.17 Of special significance is 27.79 where cow meat itself is prescribed for individuals suffering from muscular dystrophy, dry cough, or extreme illness. 18 Clearly, there’s no taboo whatsoever, and the doctors of the time were freely prescribing meat as medication without the State or clergy coming down on them with penalties. In 27.311, we also learn of meats that are not to be consumed. These include dried or putrid meat and meat from animals that died naturally, of old age, or of illness.19 Animals that are killed by other animals are also prohibited. 27.312–14 reiterate the prescription of all other meats as a source of not just basic nourishment but also great health.20 In 5.11, we are advised against long-term consumption of cow meat, implying it’s okay sporadically.21 But even here, the list includes not just cow meat but also pork, fish, buffalo meat, cheese, and even curd.22

Sushruta too agrees and in 46.352 thru 359 of Sushruta Samhita, we find enumerated several varieties of meat preparations including ullupta (minced), barjita (fries), piṣṭa (made into balls or cakes), pratāpta (roasted in ghee over charcoal), kandūpacita (dipped in mustard oil and aromatic spices, then roasted to a honey hue), pariśuṣka, and pradigdha—collectively referred to as śūlya (kabobs).23 Of these, the śūlya prepared by boiling in oil are described as heat-generating, heavy to digest, and aggravating to pitta. In contrast, those fried in clarified butter are considered light, appetizing, pleasing to the palate, and beneficial to the eyes. They are said to cool the system, subdue pitta, and impart both nourishment and gustatory delight.

Thus, the two Samhitas offer not only an extensive taxonomy of meats and their medicinal uses but also a sophisticated understanding of their preparation, digestibility, and humoral effects—reflecting a culture in which meat consumption was both scientifically analyzed and gastronomically refined.

Having said that, there’s also evidence of absolute abstinence among Buddhist and Brahmin ascetics. From the writings of Syrian Gnostic poet Bardesanes, we learn that an embassy of Indian gymnosophists was received in the Syrian court, then part of the Roman Empire, in the early third century.24 From this embassy, whose members Bardesanes claims to have personally interacted in Mesopotamia, he learns that to these Brahmin ascetics, even touching animal food was impious and unclean.25 Do note that the term used is “ascetic.” The gymnosophists were not ordinary Brahmins or householders bound by ritual duties but renunciants who had withdrawn entirely from social and material life. These were hermits devoted to penance and contemplation, having renounced all worldly attachments—including clothing itself. The very word itself is a Greek compound of gymnós, meaning “naked,” and sophistḗs, meaning “philosopher.” One who has forsaken even the basic comfort of garments could scarcely be expected to indulge in culinary pleasures such as meat. Whether such abstinence extended to the broader Brahmin or non-Brahmin populace, however, finds no corroboration in the evidence.

Roughly coeval with the Kushans were the Satavahanas who, at their peak, controlled much of present-day Maharashtra, Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh, and Karnataka. Among key archeological activities associated with this dynasty is the ancient site of Chandrakhera, a little over 90 miles southeast of Nagpur. Material findings at this site have offered the most indicative evidence of meat consumption in the Satavahana domain. Case in point being a hoard of over two thousand skeletal fragments comprising the bones of diverse animals, including mollusks.26 When closely examined, at least some of these bones, mostly mammalian, show signs of charring and sharp cuts.27 This most notably includes bovines, more likely zebu if not cow.28 In fact, there are more bovines than even goats.29

In sum, meat was ubiquitous across the subcontinent in the early centuries of the Common Era—from Magadha in the east to Maharashtra in the west, and from Kashmir in the north to Karnataka in the south. Yet, alongside this widespread consumption, a discernible moral unease had begun to surface, especially within ascetic communities and with regard to specific meats, most notably that of the cow. Again, could this be informed by the Shramanic traditions of nonviolence and renunciation? We’ll see, but first some light on the most landmark period of India’s Antiquity, the Guptas.

The Golden Age

The Gupta Era officially marks the tail end of Indian Antiquity, its fall roughly coinciding with that of Rome. This is the period when Indo-Roman trade hit its peak and Vaishnavism experienced mainstream patronage, although the regime is known to be tolerant and even accepting of all cults including Buddhism and Jainism. For this period, the earliest account of dietary practices comes from the Jaina work Angavijja. Further glimpses of food habits appear in the Mṛcchakaṭika, and the Bṛhat Saṃhitā, and the works of Kalidasa. The first continuous record, however, comes from Chinese pilgrims like Faxian, Xuanzang, and Yijing. Medical treatises such as the Aṣṭāṅga Saṃgraha, Aṣṭāṅga Hṛdaya, and the Bower Manuscript repeat much from earlier texts but add new insights, while the Puranas and Smritis shed light on the era’s dietary customs, feasts, and etiquette. Long story short, there’s much we can glean from. Let’s start with the Chinese gaze.

Faxian or Fa-Hien was an early Buddhist pilgrim who visited India during the reign of Chandragupta II, in the early years of the fifth century. By 412 AD, he was back in China and had already recorded his Indian experiences at length in one of world’s oldest travelogues, Foguo Ji, better known as “A Record of Buddhist Kingdoms.” In this work, he mostly speaks of the Middle Country or Majjhima Deśa. Contrary to what one would guess, this referred to the core area of the Magadha kingdom and nearby regions. and not the Central India of today. It included cities like Mathura, Bodh Gaya, Sarnath, and Rajagriha.

In this Middle Country, Faxian tells us, there were no butcher shops in the cities and the only ones in the business were outcastes known as chandala, who lived outside the city gates.30 This was his experience in Mathura.31 What’s intriguing, however, is that if no city-dweller partook in meat and only the chandala could slaughter animals and sell their meat, who was buying the meat? Clearly there was some degree of taboo attached to meat-eating, strong enough to relegate its trade to outside the city walls. But just as clearly, not strong enough to ban its consumption entirely. Like we treat alcohol and tobacco today? Or like in some parts we treat meat itself today? Of course, a single source is far from enough to draw lasting conclusions. So, let’s look at some more.

By the time of the Guptas, we start running into a healthy volume of secular writing. These works, devoid of religious encumbrances, can offer an excellent insight into how the society of the time was rather than how it was prescribed to be. A most notable name in that context is Kalidasa. Among his works is a Sanskrit play titled Mālavikāgnimitram. It narrates the story of King Agnimitra’s love for the exiled handmaiden Mālavikā, whose hidden royal birth eventually secures her position as his queen. Agnimitra was the son and heir of none other than Pushyamitra Sunga.

Toward the end of Act II of this five-act play, is a conversation between the king and his court jester, Vidushaka. At one point in this conversation, the jester likens the love-struck king to a vulture circling a butcher’s shop, which both amuses and vexes the king:32

bhavaṃ vi sūṇāparisaracaro vi’a giddho āmisaloluo bhīruo a.
“You too, like a bird hovering over a slaughterhouse, are desirous of meat.” (trans. Mishra)

Again, sure this could be a slaughterhouse outside the city walls, but the fact that it exists, could indicate consumption. Now, one could reason that even the customers of such slaughterhouses could be outcastes but equally reasonable would be the assumption that they could be city-dwellers. The fact that Kalidasa even invokes that analogy does favor this assumption.

Another work from the period is Mṛcchakatika, “The Little Clay Cart,” a ten-act Sanskrit play by one Shudraka, who self-identifies as a Kshatriya king in the prologue. This play is set in Avanti of the first millennium BC. Here, in the fourth act, a Brahmin jester is escorted by Vasantasena’s maid through the opulent abode of the wealthy and beautiful courtesan. Along the way, the Brahmin is shown, among other things, a butcher boy washing the entrails of a slaughtered animal:33

ayamaparaḥ paṭaccaramiva hatapaśūdarapēśiṃ dhāvati rūpidārakaḥ.
“Here is this butcher’s boy washing the entrails of a killed animal, looking like (a piece) of an old garment.” (trans. Kale)

While not a Brahmin, the courtesan is certainly not an outcaste, and her abode is most certainly within the city walls. Her opulent way of life, albeit frowned upon in elite circles, is by no means compatible with that of an Untouchable who is receiving a Brahmin at her place. In the tenth act, the villain Shakara announces that he lives on a diet of rice, meat, and fish:34

māṃsena tiktāmlena bhaktaṃ śākena sūpena samatsyakena,
bhuktaṃ mayā’tmano gehe śālīyakūreṇa guḍaudanena.
“In my palace, I have dined on rice, and mat seasoned with pungent and sour sauces, and vegetables, soup and fish, and rice-cakes and treacled rice.” (trans. Kane)

This time, it’s a Kshatriya we’re talking about. Shakara is the brother-in-law of Ujjain’s King Pālaka. Now we have a most unambiguous instance of a non-outcaste consuming meat without any fear of ostracism. Although the play is set in the pre-Common Era, it’s composed in the fifth century AD, hence likely more reflective of the later period than the former.

Coming back to Kalidasa, we also have a meat reference in his Abhijnana Shakuntalam. A dramatized retelling of the story of Shakuntala from the Mahabharata, this seven-act play is held as Kalidasa’s magnum opus. Its second act opens with a long monologue by a jester. It’s in this monologue that we find the reference to meat:35

aniyatavēlaṃ śulyamāṃsa bhūyiśṭha āhārо̄ bhujyatē.
“At irregular hours we eat our meals which are mostly comprised of flesh roasted on a spit.” (trans. Kale)

Although the jester is quite likely a Shudra, notice the use of “we” in his monologue. He’s referring to himself together with his master, the King, who is undoubtedly a Kshatriya. Once again, meat is consumed. If there’s a taboo, it certainly isn’t strong enough to prevent a Kshatriya from indulging.

The Vedic practice of including meat in funerary offerings seems to have continued through this period too. We can confirm this from a careful reading of the later interpolations of various Puranas, including Vayu, Matsya, and Kurma. And yet it’s undeniable that the society was no longer as accepting of meat as it used to be. Even a work like Kamasutra, another landmark writing of the era, names carnivory as one of the acts that run against dharma.36 In the post-Gupta period, at the cusp of the Medieval Era, we start getting a much clearer picture thanks to an increased influx of foreign pilgrims like Yijing and Xuanzang, which makes the prevalence of meat less of a guesswork than ever before.

Early Medieval India

By the 8th century, the Guptas had long been supplanted by new principalities that had sprouted all over the subcontinent from its remnants. The era of the Indic empires was now over. The literary output of this period gives us a fairly detailed idea of how India ate at the time. A few Puranas were also composed in this period, although the bulk of the Puranic corpus belongs in the Kushan-Gupta era. Part the earliest secular literature from the post-Gupta times is a lesser-known Jain work by the name Samarāiccakahā. It was composed by Acharya Haribhadrasuri, a Jain mendicant understood to be from Tamil Nadu.

One of the most striking episodes in the Samarāiccakahā recounts the fate of a prosperous merchant who, despite dying in meditation—a state ordinarily associated with spiritual attainment—is reborn as a pig.37 In a grim twist of karmic irony, the animal ends up captured and butchered for a feast in his own palace kitchen.38 This was done to replace the meat that the cooks had already prepared earlier but was carried away by a cat.39 A wealthy merchant in the early medieval era is more likely to be a Vaishya than a Shudra. In another episode, a man named Dharana, desperate to save his ailing wife, offers her the flesh of a rabbit that had perished in a forest fire.40 This Dharana too is a merchant, which makes him unlikely to be an outcaste.41

Although many foreigners visited India in this period, we’ll look at one particular visitor from China for our purposes. His name is Yijing or I-Tsing. He spent some fourteen years in the subcontinent, most of which were at the monastery in Nalanda. In his accounts, Yijing gives us some elaboration of the Indian gastronomy as he witnessed. From this we learn that food here was divided into two categories of five each—panchabhoganiyam and panchakhadaniyam.42 The former is items that should be swallowed and the latter, that should be chewed. Of the five in his bhoganiyam list is meat.43 Which is rather strange given how difficult it is to consume without chewing.44 Does this mean meat was commonplace? In and of itself, no. Because there’s no other mention thereof anyplace else in the work.

Almost half a century before Yijing, India was visited by Hiuen Tsang, also known as Xuanzang. He came during the reign of King Harsha whose dynasty had risen from the remnants of the Gupta Empire. Harsha is remembered for his generosity and for his campaigns against the Huns, which he undertook together with the Maukharis. During his sixteen-year stay in India, Hiuen Tsang traveled widely across the subcontinent, visiting major centers of learning such as Nalanda and documenting his experiences in great detail. Upon his return to China, he composed a monumental account titled Da Tang Xiyu Ji or “The Great Tang Records on the Western Regions.” In this work, he described the political landscape, social customs, religious practices, and educational institutions of India with remarkable precision. His observations remain among the most valuable sources for understanding the India of Harsha’s time.

From this work we learn that items like fish and mutton were commonplace in the Gangetic Plains.45 But we also learn that this diet was mostly confined to Kshatriyas and other non-Brahmin, non-Buddhist communities.46 Also, even where meat was acceptable, animals like oxen, horses, donkeys, pigs, dogs, apes, monkeys, etc. were already taboo.47 We will discuss this work a little more when we explore the Shramana diets, but for now, let’s move on to some Sanskrit works from the period.

A work from the cusp of ninth and tenth centuries is Kavyamimamsa. Although authored by the Rajashekhara, a Maharashtri Prakrit poet, this prescriptive treatise on poetry is in Sanskrit. One of its verses does discuss a specific kind of meat—pork:48

ikṣudaṇḍasya maṇḍasya dadhnaḥ piṣṭakṛtasya ca,
vārāhasya ca māṃsasya saiṣa gacchati phālgunaḥ.
“Sugarcane, rice gruel, yogurt, things made from black gram flour (fritters, cakes, savories, and such), and the flesh of the wild boar—this month of Phalguna, fit for the consumption of these things, is passing away.” (trans. Saraswat)

Here, we learn that meat of boar is not only accepted alongside rice and lentils but is also assigned a preferred season. While this example does not prove anything about other kinds of meat, it does confirm the presence of pork in the Hindu diet of the time. Was it widespread? Was it common in all Hindu diets or only that of certain castes? These questions remain unanswered.

For some reason, much of the literature from this era is from Jain writers. We have already seen Samarāiccakahā. From the same century we also Somadeva Suri’s retelling of King Yashodhara’s story in his Yashastilaka. In this story, a ritual involving the sacrifice of a rooster is completed using a model of the bird fashioned out of flour. This way, the gods are propitiated without necessitating an actual slaughter, which is increasingly seen as a cruel practice even in the Puranic interpolations of the time. For example, Bhagavata Purana in 7.15.7 clearly forbids any animal food in funerary rites:49

na dadyādāmiṣaṃ śrāddhe na cādyāddharmatattvavit,
munyannaiḥ syātparā prītiryathā na paśuhiṃsayā.
“In Shraddha ho should not offer flesh nor should he at all eat it, if he knows the truth of Dharma, what supreme gratification arises from the simple fare of a hermit cannot be had by killing animals.” (trans. Rau)

This is interesting because elsewhere, the Puranic corpus seems clearly more accommodating. For instance, Padma Purana in 3.56.31b, gives an exhaustive list of creatures that must not be consumed as food and those that could, reminiscent of similar lists in the earlier Brahmana literature.50 But before we conclude a conclusive and definitive shift away from carnivory, we also can’t miss, for example, the Naishadha Charita from the 12th century. The work is counted among the five mahakavyas of the Sanskrit canon and held in high esteem. And there’s copious mention of meat as an acceptable and even desirable part of the diet, at least among the wealthy. In its sixteenth canto, there’s a royal feat that involves meat dressed as the bimba fruit.51 In another verse the same feast also features meats of deer,52 goats, and birds.53

The most generous mention of meat comes from the landmark socio-cultural commentary from the twelfth century named Manasollasa. The name itself means “the entertainer of the mind,” which already hints at its endorsement of epicurean liberties. This text was authored by the Chalukyan king Someshvara and therefore must be seen in that context, i.e. from the lens of the royalty rather than the lay practitioner of the faith.

In 3.1430 thru 1435, a pig is roasted on fire and cut into śuṇṭha, pieces of meat as per Monier-Williams,54 which is then roasted again on charcoals and subsequently eaten with rock salt and black pepper.55 In the verses that follow, 1436 thru 1441, the śuṇṭhas are blended into sweetened curd along with lemon pulp and fumigated to prepare a dish called cakkālikā.56 Further down, in verse 1447, we encounter pieces of meat made into broth and perfumed with aromatic spices.57 The chapter abounds in references to an astonishing variety of meat preparations, including kavacandī, puryalā, bhadiṭraka, kṛṣṇapāka, vaṭaka, bhūṣikā, kośalī, pañcavarṇī, and maṇḍalīya. Clearly, meat is relished at royal banquets and remains the pinnacle of epicurean delight.

The only conclusion one can safely draw from this is that while it was increasingly frowned upon and even discouraged strongly in certain quarters, there still existed pockets of non-outcaste communities that enjoyed a meat diet with little apology. These were mostly the Kshatrya royalties. One must also remember that this is a huge subcontinent. And for a landmass of this size and diversity, it’s not unlikely that even religious injunctions would have varied by the region and socioeconomic status. Hinduism, it would seem, was far from a unitary monolith even back in the day. Now the question one is tempted to ask is, whence this shift away from carnivory?

If our Vedic ancestors had little compunction consuming meat of all kinds, including, by the way, beef, why this growing revulsion in the Common Era? Many scholars pin this on Buddhism and Jainism. Before we conclude this conversation, therefore, it’s only fair that we throw some light on the Shramanic attitude toward meat diets.

The Shramana Pragmatism

Let’s return to the pin we dropped on Hiuen Tsang in the last section. Since he spent much of his time among the Buddhist monks at Nalanda, his observations hold special meaning in the context of the Shramanic diet. We have already learned that meat was consumed, albeit with much reservation among Kshatriyas and other communities of the time. But what about the Shramanas?

One particularly interesting observation concerns the Sarvāstivādin monks at a Hīnayāna monastery. Xuanzang notes that these monks were “pure and strict” in observing the Vinaya rules and that they consumed three kinds of “pure meat.”58 He does not elaborate on what these meats were or what rendered them “pure.” Although the monastery he describes was located not in India but in Yanqi in China,59 the very fact that practicing Buddhist monks partook of meat deemed “pure” is noteworthy. The same is repeated in the context of yet another Hinayana monastery in Qiuci or Kucha, an ancient Buddhist kingdom on the northern edge of the Taklamakan Desert.60 Interestingly, he does not say much about the diet of Buddhist monks in India other than the fact that they, along with Brahmins, abstained from alcohol. One is free to draw their conclusions at this point but for a fuller picture, it’d help to explore the absolute roots of the tradition.

Going back to works of a more scriptural nature, we have several mentions of meat in the Jataka tales. In 537, titled Mahā-Sutasoma-jātaka, we have a king of Kashi who would not eat rice without meat.61 The specific type of meat is not mentioned, but the text makes it clear that it was an essential part of his meal. As the story progresses, the king turns to human flesh and subsequently ends up getting exiled to the forest where he’s to spend the rest of his days as a man-eating ogre. The story ends with him repenting, breaking free from the curse, and resolving to give up human flesh. Of note is that although he gives up human flesh at the behest of none other than the Buddha, he doesn’t renounce flesh altogether. Does this mean meat was acceptable to the Buddha?

There are several more meat references scattered throughout the Jatakas. In 408, for instance, we find a meat-market in the city of Mithila.62 In 292, we have fish being prepared in the royal kitchen of Kashi.63 And elsewhere we have meats of deer, wild boar, and several kinds of birds being referenced in various contexts. In 144, the Bodhisattva is born in a Brahmin family and upon coming of age, he chooses to renounce the family life in quest of Enlightenment. In the forest, he attempts to sacrifice an ox to the Fire God but stops for want of salt.64 While at first crack this may seem like an endorsement of beef, a fuller reading shows it to be a moral parable on the futility of ritual sacrifice rather than a sanction thereof.

All these examples confirm, at the very least, that meat was very much a part of the diet in those days even though slaughter and sacrifice of animals was not particularly endorsed by the Buddha. But we still have much to scour. Let’s bring in the canon this time.

A central component of the Buddhist canon is the Vinaya Pitaka, which outlines the rules, discipline, and conduct governing the monastic community, and is divided into sections such as the Suttavibhanga, Mahavagga, and Cullavagga, which combine procedural codes, ethical guidelines, and illustrative narratives. Together, these divisions maintain the cohesion, moral integrity, and orderly functioning of the early Sangha. And meat marks its presence here as well.

In Mahavagga 6.10, the Buddha offers one of the clearest approvals of meat consumption. A monk, suffering from an illness, follows the Buddha’s advice to visit a slaughterhouse and consume uncooked pork, which restores his health. The Buddha then declares, “I allow, O Bhikkhus, in the case of a disease not human, the use of raw flesh and of blood.”65 This isn’t even the only such declaration. In the same chapter or khandika, we have the same reiterated in 6.15.66 In 6.23 is a curious incident involving a monk seeking meat through alms. A sick Bhikkhu requests meat broth from a woman to ease his illness.67 She instructs her servant to procure meat from the market, but the servant returns empty-handed because cattle slaughter is prohibited in the kingdom that day.68 In response, the woman removes a piece of her own flesh and has the servant deliver it to the Bhikkhu.69 Later, the Buddha rebukes the monk—not for consuming meat, but for failing to inquire about its source.70 This episode reveals two points: cattle slaughter was not universally taboo, hence the occasional and not universal ban, and the Buddha permitted meat consumption, provided it was not human. But how does such allowance of carnivory reconcile with the Buddhist idea of nonviolence anyway? And why does the Buddha seem so ambivalent on the subject?

Another key text of the Pali Canon is the Sutta Nipāta, a collection of poems and verses imparting ethical teachings, meditation guidance, and reflections on impermanence, compassion, and the path to liberation. In verses 2.2.3 thru 2.2.9, it records Kassapa, a Buddha preceding Gautama, explaining that while killing is sinful, consuming meat is not.71 Also, curiously, he’s addressed as a Brahmin himself,72 indicating the fluid interchange between Brahminical and Shramanic identities. This section could answer the question we just asked, about the compatibility of meat diets with Buddhist nonviolence. But how does one get meat without killing? Isn’t that a little hypocritical in some sense?

The picture gets clearer with Vinaya’s Mahavagga 31.12 thru 14. In these sections, it’s Siddhartha Gautama, the last Buddha, himself who partakes in the controversial diet. So, the story goes that Sīha, a Licchavi general, offered meat to the Buddha at his own request, and the Buddha accepted and ate it.73 The Nigaṇṭhas then accused him of consuming flesh from an animal slain for him. In response, the Buddha clarified that meat eating was permissible so long as the animal was not killed for that purpose, allowing it if the eater had neither seen, heard, nor suspected the killing.74 This is the most unambiguous canonical instance establishing that the Buddha not only ate meat but also explicitly sanctioned its consumption under defined ethical conditions, distinguishing intent and knowledge of killing as the determining factors of moral culpability.

Two curious points emerge from this episode. First, it stands in direct contrast to the Brahminical view, which expressly forbade meat from animals not killed for the purpose—a position clearly articulated in Kautilya’s Arthashastra 2.26.1275 and, as we’ve already seen before, in the Charaka Samhita. Second, even the Buddha’s own conduct invites debate, for his accusers, the Nigaṇṭhas, were none other than the followers of Mahavira, the Jains.

Speaking of Jains, we find no exceptions whatsoever to their notion of nonviolence anyplace in their canon. Such is the unremitting nature of this stricture that it prohibits meat even when starved or prescribed by a vaidya. This is one Shramana group that truly shuns violence in all forms.

Conclusion

Across two millennia the subcontinent never spoke with one voice. Rigvedic poets relished barbequed steers. Kautilya’s spies counted carcasses. Gupta courtiers roasted boar while ascetics renounced meat entirely. Manu, ever the lawyer, hedged that meat is evil, meat is meritorious, meat is permitted when the Brahmin requests, and forbidden when he does not. Buddhist monks ate “un-suspected” pork. Jain mendicants starved rather than bruise a worm. Physicians prescribed beef for wasting disease. Playwrights sent butchers’ boys coursing through courtesans’ mansions. Foreign eyes saw cities without butcher shops yet country feasts where goat, deer, rhino, and peacock circled the Phalguna moon.

Yet toward the beginning of the Common Era a clear shift emerged in the Indian attitude toward meat. The change was not sudden but the result of many overlapping influences. The rise of renunciatory ideals, the growing power of non-violent sects, and the relatively stronger Buddhist and absolute Jain injunctions against killing all contributed to a gradual softening of the cultural palate. Vegetarianism took shape less as an external conquest by new faiths and more as an internal Brahminical strategy to out-ascetic the ascetics while keeping ritual control over the occasional consecrated morsel. The cow, long held sacred even when its killing was tolerated, became the unmistakable line no orthodox householder would cross. Her protection turned into a symbol of civic orthodoxy in an age of mingled courts and mixed cuisines. Indians consistently preferred other varieties of meat over beef. Thus, Hindu India reached its modern reputation as a civilization that worships the animal it once ate and that celebrates abstinence while never quite forgetting the taste of the skewer.

truncated_body_text: In a previous article we studied the Vedic and post-Vedic positions on meat as food before the Common Era. We concluded with the understanding that meat was far more ubiquitous in the Indian diet at the time than we like to admit today. That the injunction on meat was really a spectrum with some verses prohibiting it all, others allowing some, and yet others allowing it all. Precisely as it is today.

publication.name: Schandillia
publication.hero_text: Thrilling, well-referenced dives into Indian and global history—mostly Indian—with a dash of serious myth-busting, that challenges what you always held as self-evident. In short, history with the rigor of science.
